skill.name: zinc-database
skill.description: Access ZINC compound data for virtual screening, analog discovery, supplier lookup, and docking-ready structure retrieval.
skill.summary: Find purchasable ZINC compounds, analogs, and docking-ready structures.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, C2, SQLite, and weak-crypto patterns. Review confirms the command and network findings are documentation examples for ZINC API access and molecular docking workflows, while the blocker alerts are keyword false positives with no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection. Publish with a warning because examples can make external requests, write files, and download large datasets.

medium_findings.title: Network and Local Command Examples Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The static external command and network findings are true for the documentation examples, but they are not hidden skill code. The skill instructs users to run curl, wget, aria2c, gunzip, obabel, dock6, vina, and Python subprocess examples against ZINC and local files. This is legitimate for a ZINC database skill, but it can send query data to external services and download large files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain concrete shell and subprocess examples that make external network requests and write downloaded data locally. The risk is contextual because the examples are documentation, not automatically executed code.

medium_findings.title: Large File Download and Local Processing Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that redirect API results to local files, recursively download ZINC tranche files, decompress archives, and convert molecular files. This is expected for docking workflows, but users should confirm storage impact and trusted paths before running commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly create output files and download or transform molecule archives. This is normal for the domain but has resource and filesystem side effects.

low_findings.title: C2, Cryptography, and SQLite Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The high blocker alerts do not show malicious command-and-control behavior, credential access, or weak cryptographic implementation. They appear to match ordinary ZINC version text, DOCK database file extensions, molecular docking command names, and citation text. No prompt injection language was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms occur in scientific database documentation and docking file names, not in code that controls systems or implements cryptography. Targeted injection searches found no suspicious override or system-instruction language.

low_findings.title: Python Curl Wrappers Build URLs From Inputs
low_findings.description: The Python examples build ZINC query URLs from SMILES, ZINC IDs, and output fields before passing them to curl with subprocess.run. The subprocess calls use argument lists rather than a shell, so command injection evidence was not found, but URL encoding and input validation should be added when adapting the examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is real, but shell injection is mitigated by list arguments and no shell=True evidence was found. The remaining concern is malformed URLs or unintended data disclosure through external queries.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network and Local Command Examples Require User Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The static external command and network findings are true for the documentation examples, but they are not hidden skill code. The skill instructs users to run curl, wget, aria2c, gunzip, obabel, dock6, vina, and Python subprocess examples against ZINC and local files. This is legitimate for a ZINC database skill, but it can send query data to external services and download large files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain concrete shell and subprocess examples that make external network requests and write downloaded data locally. The risk is contextual because the examples are documentation, not automatically executed code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python Curl Wrappers Build URLs From Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python examples build ZINC query URLs from SMILES, ZINC IDs, and output fields before passing them to curl with subprocess.run. The subprocess calls use argument lists rather than a shell, so command injection evidence was not found, but URL encoding and input validation should be added when adapting the examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is real, but shell injection is mitigated by list arguments and no shell=True evidence was found. The remaining concern is malformed URLs or unintended data disclosure through external queries.

content.user_title: Search ZINC Compounds for Drug Discovery
content.value_statement: Drug discovery teams need fast access to purchasable compounds and docking-ready molecular structures. This skill guides ZINC searches, analog discovery, supplier lookups, and structure retrieval workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to search ZINC by ZINC ID, SMILES, supplier code, and random sampling parameters.
Provides CartBlanche22 API endpoint formats and output field guidance for ZINC22 queries.
Shows workflows for analog discovery, docking library preparation, and chemical space sampling.
Documents tranche codes, molecular property interpretation, and file repository organization.
Includes Python and command-line examples for parsing results and downloading 3D structure files.
limitations: It does not provide a live API connector or authenticated access layer by itself.
ZINC availability, supplier catalogs, and API responses can change outside the skill.
Large 3D structure downloads can require significant storage and network bandwidth.
Users must validate chemical structures and procurement decisions independently.

use_cases.title: Find analogs for a hit compound
use_cases.description: Search ZINC by SMILES and tune similarity distance values to identify purchasable analog candidates.

use_cases.title: Prepare a virtual screening set
use_cases.description: Select lead-like, drug-like, or fragment subsets and retrieve fields needed for docking preparation.

use_cases.title: Map supplier codes to ZINC records
use_cases.description: Use catalog item searches to connect vendor identifiers with ZINC IDs, SMILES strings, and catalogs.

prompt_templates.title: Look up one compound
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ZINC database skill to explain how I can retrieve SMILES, catalog, and tranche fields for ZINC000000000001.

prompt_templates.title: Search for analogs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ZINC database skill to design a SMILES similarity search for my hit compound. Include distance settings and useful output fields.

prompt_templates.title: Build a docking set
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ZINC database skill to plan a lead-like docking library workflow with ZINC IDs, SMILES, tranche filters, and 3D structure retrieval steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a batch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ZINC database skill to outline a batch retrieval workflow for 500 ZINC IDs, including rate limits, URL encoding, parsing, caching, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: Find purchasable analogs for an ibuprofen-like SMILES string.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend a SMILES similarity search, starting with a narrow distance value and expanding only if results are sparse. It would request ZINC ID, SMILES, catalog, and tranche fields for review.

output_examples.input: Prepare compounds for a docking screen.
output_examples.output: The skill would outline subset selection, result field selection, tranche interpretation, 3D file retrieval, decompression, and structure validation before docking.

output_examples.input: Check whether supplier catalog codes map to ZINC entries.
output_examples.output: The skill would guide catalog item searches and recommend cross-checking supplier availability before ordering or building a screening library.

best_practices: Start with exact or narrow searches before broad similarity searches to reduce noise and data transfer.
Request only the output fields needed for the current workflow.
Validate SMILES, tranche properties, and supplier availability before experimental use.
anti_patterns: Do not run recursive downloads without confirming storage, bandwidth, and scope.
Do not assume supplier availability is current without independent verification.
Do not use unencoded SMILES strings in production API workflows.

faq.question: What ZINC version does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on ZINC22 while also noting ZINC20 and ZINC15 differences.

faq.question: Can it retrieve compounds by SMILES?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains exact and similarity searches using SMILES and distance parameters.

faq.question: Does it download 3D structures automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and workflows that users can review before running.

faq.question: Is it suitable for molecular docking workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers docking library preparation, tranche files, and common structure formats.

faq.question: Does it verify compound quality?
faq.answer: No. Users must validate structures, supplier status, and experimental suitability.

faq.question: Why does the security audit mention network risk?
faq.answer: The skill includes examples that query public ZINC endpoints and download files from ZINC repositories.
